content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Donald M. Jensen, P.E. 
C.E.O. Jensen Design and Survey, Inc. 
1672 Donlon Street 
Ventura, CA 93003 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-13-138 
Dear Mr. Jensen: 
November 15, 2013 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair 
Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when 
rendering advice. (/11 re Oglesby (1975), 1 FPPC Ops. 7 l.) Please note that all advice is based 
only on the provisions of the Act. We offer no opinion on the application of other laws that may 
apply, such as Government Code Section 1090 or common law conflict of interest. Also, the 
Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) 
Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have 
already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective 
actions. 
QUESTION 
May you prepare a Municipal Assessment District Engineers Report for a project that 
covers the development of your real property? 
CONCLUSION 
No. As discussed below, it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will 
have a material financial effect on your real property. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9l014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18 I 10 through 18 997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-13-138 
Page No. 2 
You are an engineer in the city of Ventura and you own a private engineering firm. Your 
firm contracts with the City on an occasional basis lo provide engineering design and other 
services. 
You own approximately four acres of real property subdivided, zoned, and prepared for 
residential development in Ventura. The subdivision has all discretionary approvals, and a 
vesting tentative map is fully approved. One required condition for recordation of the final map 
is the City's formation and acceptance of a Maintenance Assessment District. To create these 
districts, the City contracts with an engineering consulting firm to draft a Maintenance 
Assessment District Report (MAD Report). The MAD Report establishes a district around the 
proposed project, calculates and distributes various costs for public land, taxes, and legal 
compliance with various sections of local and state law. You indicate that acceptance of a MAD 
Report is assumed, and that rejection is not an option for the City. 
A MAD Report must be drafted, and it is lo your financial benefit that you prepare it. 
You prepare such reports as a matter of course for your clients, have extensive knowledge of the 
process and this particular parcel of land, and can prepare for a fraction of the cost to engage a 
different firm. You use industry standard prices and costs when drafting MAD Reports. This 
report will be based on previously accepted MAD Reports, including recently accepted reports, 
and one MAD Report that you prepared for this same property 12 years ago. 
The City Attorney determines that any firm that contracts with the city to prepare a MAD 
Report is a "consultant" for the purposes of the Act. However, you have not been required to file 
Form 700, a Statement of Economic Interests. Based on this designation as a consultant, the City 
Attorney has informed you that you will have a disqualifying conflict of interest that prohibits 
you from preparing the Report, even in your private capacity. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official ha,; a financial interest in a governmental decision, within the 
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the public official's interests a,; set forth in Section 87103. (See also, 
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding 
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a governmental decision: 
Step One: Are you a "public official"? 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials. (Sections 87100, 
87103; Regulation 18700(b )(I).) A public official is "every member, officer, employee or 
consultant of a state or local government agency .... " (Section 82048.) 


File No. A-13-138 
Page No. 3 
In determining whether you are a consultant under the Act, Regulation 1870l(a)(2)(A) 
indicates that an individual is a consultant if, pursuant to a contract, he or she makes a 
governmental decision whether to: 
(i) Approve a rate or regulation; 
(ii) Adopt or enforce a law; 
(iii) Issue, deny, suspend, or revoke and permit, license, application, certificate, approval, 
order, or similar authorization or entitlement; 
(iv) Authorize the City to enter into, modify, or renew a contract provided it is the type of 
contract that requires agency approval; 
(v) Grant agency approval to a contract that requires agency approval and to which the 
agency is a party, or to the specifications for such a contract; 
(vi) Grant agency approval to a plan, design, report, study or similar item; 
(vii) Adopt, or grant agency approval of policies, standards, or guidelines for the City. 
You have stated that you will be preparing the report at the City's request, and that 
rejection is not an option. The report designates policies, standards, or guidelines for the City. 
This means that your report carries the weight of adopting a policy, standard, or guideline for the 
City. You are consultant under the Act. 
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental 
decision? 
A public official makes a governmental decision when, acting within the authority of his 
or her position, the official votes on a matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her 
agency to any particular course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of 
his or her agency, or determines not to act. (Regulation 18702.1 (a).) A public official 
participates in a governmental decision when, acting within the authority of his or her position, 
the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the 
governmental decision without substantive research or investigation. (Regulation 18702.2.) A 
public official uses his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if, for the 
purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or 
consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation l 8702.3(a).) In preparing the MAD Report at the 
City's request, you will be preparing policies, standards, or guidelines that they will not reject. 
Preparing the report is making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision. 
Step Three: What are your financial interests? 
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial interest in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effects on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 


immediate family, or on any of the following five enumerated interests: 
File No. A-13-138 
Page No. 4 
• An interest in any business entity if the official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1 (a)), or if the official is a director, 
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management in the business 
entity. (Section 87103.l(d); Regulation 18703.l(b)). 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.) 
• An interest in a source of non-gift income or promised income, which aggregates to $500 
or more within the 12 months prior to the governmental decision. (Section 87103(c); 
Regulation 18703.3.) 
• An interest in any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of gifts that 
aggregate to a fair market value of $440 or more within 12 months prior to the 
governmental decision. (Section 87103(e). Regulation 18703.4.) 
• An interest in the official's personal finances or the finances of the official's immediate 
family. The "personal financial effects" rule finds interest where a governmental 
decision will increase or decrease the personal income, expenses, assets, or liabilities of 
the official or their immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation l 8703.5.) 
Real Property: 
By all indications, your property interest is valued at $2,000 or more. If so, you have a 
potentially disqualifying interest in real property. (Section 87103(b).) Because you have not 
provided facts indicating any other potential financial interest, our analysis is limited to your real 
property interest. 
Business Interest: 
By all indications, you have an ownership interest of at least 10% in your company, and 
that ownership interest is worth $2,000 or more. You therefore have an interest in the business 
entity under Section 87103(d). However, the facts presented do not indicate that your business is 
the subject of your decisions with respect to the MAD Report drafting. Without further 
indication regarding the process by which an engineering firm is selected, we cannot analyze 
potential conflicts that may arise because of your business. 
You also have an interest in clients who provide, based on a proportionate share of your 
business income, $500 or more in income to you during a 12-month period prior to your 
participation in the MAD Report drafting. You have not provided sufficient facts for us to 
analyze these potential sources for conflicts of interest. As mentioned, we cannot analyze 
potential conflicts that may arise because of your business. 


Source of Income: 
File No. A-13-138 
Page No. 5 
It also appears that you have received income from your business of $500 or more in the 
last 12 months prior to the MAD Report drafting. If so, you will have an interest in your 
business as a source of income. (Section 87103(c).) As with the analysis of your business 
interest, we cannot analyze this potential conflict without more information regarding the process 
by which an engineering firm is selected for MAD Report drafting. 
Personal Finances: 
A public official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. A governmental 
decision will have an effect on this interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, 
income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or 
decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) However, a financial effect on the gross 
revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which a public official 
has a direct or indirect investment interest is not considered a separate financial effect on an 
official's personal finances and would not be analyzed separately under the "personal financial 
effects" rule. (Regulation 18705.S(a).) 
It is unclear from the facts whether the savings incurred by the use of your own 
engineering firm will benefit your personal finances or those of a business entity. While we 
cannot analyze your specific situation, we can offer general information regarding this potential 
conflict. 
Step Four: Is your real property interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision? 
To determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a 
given interest is material, you must first determine if the interest is directly involved or indirectly 
involved in the governmental decision. (Regulation I 8704(a).) 
Real Property: 
Regulation 18704.2 provides, in part: 
"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is 
directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply: 
"(I) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of 
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries ( or the 
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the 
governmental decision." 


File No. A-13-138 
Page No. 6 
Your property is within the MAD, and is therefore within 500 feet of the proposed 
boundaries. Accordingly, unless an exception applies, your real property interest is directly 
involved in the real property governmental decisions regarding the MAD Report under 
Regulation 18704.2(a)( 1 ). 
In your request, you mention several exceptions that you think may apply in your case. 
The Commission construes exceptions to the general conflict of interest rules narrowly. (See 
Odennan Advice Letter, No. A-00-082 and Torrance Advice Letter, No. A-94-084.) We do not 
see that any exceptions apply to the facts you provide. 
Regulation 18702.4(a)(3) allows a consultant to participate in a governmental decision 
regarding his or her compensation in a private capacity. Distinguishing private capacity does not 
exempt your participation, but merely distinguishes between the different hats that you would 
need to wear when participating in a governmental decision. Applied to your facts, you would 
be making, participating in making, or using your position to influence a decision if you take part 
in drafting the MAD Report, but would not be involved with governmental decisions when you 
are solely representing yourself as the property owner. (Reed Advice Letter, No. 1-13-091.) 
Regulation 18702.4(b)(I) allows officials to privately act in their personal interests. 
These regulations allow public officials to participate in governmental decisions as any private 
citizen would. It does not give public officials the capacity to bring their private interests into a 
governmental decision. Under this exception, you must limit your participation to your personal 
interests and make clear that you are not speaking in the interest of any person or group, nor are 
you acting in your official capacity. (Mitchell Advice Letter, No. A-12-011.) For instance, in a 
matter concerning a City Councilmember's real property, that Councilmember would be 
permitted to testify at a City Council meeting as a private citizen after that member appropriately 
recuses him or herself from participating in the governmental decision in his or her public 
capacity. The exception would not allow you to be involved with the MAD Report outside of 
any public meetings. (See Mitchell Advice Letter, supra and Simonian Advice Letter, No. A-08-
096.) It allows you only to participate in public meetings in the same way as any member of the 
public would. 
Regulation l 8702.4(b )( 4) carves out an exception for architectural or engineering 
submissions and drawings. This exception is limited to the preparation of technical documents 
by public officials in their private capacity. (Grocott Advice Letter, No. A-02-028; Smith 
Advice Letter, No. A-96-022; Dorsey Advice Letter, No. A-94-284.) This exception does not 
apply to the MAD Report process, because you are preparing the MAD Report in your capacity 
as a consultant. In a memorandum to the Commission in support of the Regulation I 8702.4, 
staff explained the exception's purpose: 
"The cities were basically concerned about limitations on the professional 
activities of planning commissioners and city council members who are architects 
and engineers. There was general agreement that planning commissioner/ 
architects should not actually represent clients before the planning commission. It 


File No. A-13-138 
Page No. 7 
was also agreed that such architects and engineers should be able to prepare the 
drawings and plans for clients, even though the drawings and the plans would 
have to be submitted to the planning commission. There seemed to be general 
consensus among the city attorneys that this exception is necessary in order to 
allow the local architects and engineers to continue to practice their profession 
when they act as planning commissioners or council members." (Staff 
Memorandum to the Commission, dated April 29, 1985.) 
Based on the facts provided, Regulation I 8702.4(a)( I) may be relevant. That regulation 
provides an exception for actions of public officials that are solely ministerial, secretarial, 
manual, or clerical. If you exercise no discretion in drafting the MAD Report, then 
18702.4(a)(l) applies. Your letter indicates that there can be only one outcome with respect to 
the MAD Report. But you also indicated during our telephone conversation that there are 
industry standards by which you measure the cost of each necessary component in the 
development plans. Industry standards are not the constraints contemplated by the regulation. 
The regulation may only apply if there are constraints to the use of any discretion. Please keep 
in mind that it is irrelevant that you are legally constrained in some action by the Business and 
Professional Code. The Act's determinations regarding a conflict of interest are not informed by 
ethics enforcement mechanisms in other code sections. If you are able to exercise any discretion 
in drafting the MAD Report, then the regulation cannot apply. 
Personal Finances: 
An official's interest in his or her personal finances is deemed to be directly involved in 
the governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect on his or her personal finances. 
(Regulation 18704.5.) 
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your 
real property interest? 
A conflict-of-interest arises only where a material effect on an official's economic 
interest is reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation I 8700(a).) 
Materiality 
Real Property: 
Any financial effect, even one penny, of a governmental decision on real property 
directly involved in the governmental decision is presumed to be material. (Regulation 
I 8705.2(a)(I ).) This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable 
that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real property, irrespective of 
the impact. (Ibid.) Even a one penny change qualifies as a financial effect. Your real property is 
directly involved with the MAD Report drafting, and is presumed material. 


Personal Finances: 
File No. A-13-138 
Page No. 8 
An effect on an official's personal finances is material as stated in Regulation 
18705.S(a), which provides: 
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal finances 
is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period." 
Reasonable Foreseeability 
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to his or her 
interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the standard 
will be met. A material financial effect on an interest need not be certain or even substantially 
likely to be "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (Regulation 
18706(a); /11 re Thomer (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) 
Real Property: 
Because your property is the subject of the MAD Report, and because the MAD Report 
will distribute the costs of development and compliance to you, there is almost surely more than 
a mere possibility that drafting the MAD Report will have a one penny impact on the value of 
your real property. Unless you can prove that there will be no reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on your real property value as a result of the MAD Report, your interest will 
disqualify you from participating in drafting the Report. 
Personal Finances: 
Based upon the facts provided, you will save approximately $10,000 by using your own 
firm. It is unclear whether that money is saved by you or a business entity. If the drafting of the 
MAD report results in a benefit to your personal finances of at least $250, then your drafting the 
MAD Report will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your personal finances. 
Steps Seven and Eight: Does the governmental decision come within any exception to the 
conflict-of-interest rules? 
Public Generally 
The material financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic 
interest in real property is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if that decision 
also affects ten percent or more of all property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency, 
or 5,000 property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency. (Regulation 18707.1 (a); 
Regulation 18707.1 (b )(B)(i)-(ii).) 


File No. A-13-138 
Page No. 9 
The contemplated MAD encompasses and affects only the four acres that you own and a 
very small amount of public land. The public generally exception does not apply. 
Legally Required Participation 
There are a limited number of circumstances that allow a public official to take part in a 
governmental decision despite a disqualifying conflict of interest under the legally required 
participation exception. This exception applies very rarely, and only where the government 
agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section 8710 l; Regulation 18708.) 
You have indicated that there are at least two other engineering firms that are capable of 
completing the MAD Report, albeit at significantly higher cost. There is no prohibition based on 
cost. The legally required participation exception does not apply. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
NLS:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
Genera - -
By: 
Nicholas Sanders 
Legal Intern, Legal Division

sections.question:
May you prepare a Municipal Assessment District Engineers Report for a project that 
covers the development of your real property?
sections.conclusion:
No. As discussed below, it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will 
have a material financial effect on your real property. 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18 I 10 through 18 997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official ha,; a financial interest in a governmental decision, within the 
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the public official's interests a,; set forth in Section 87103. (See also, 
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding 
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a governmental decision: 
Step One: Are you a "public official"? 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials. (Sections 87100, 
87103; Regulation 18700(b )(I).) A public official is "every member, officer, employee or 
consultant of a state or local government agency .... " (Section 82048.)  
In determining whether you are a consultant under the Act, Regulation 1870l(a)(2)(A) 
indicates that an individual is a consultant if, pursuant to a contract, he or she makes a 
governmental decision whether to: 
(i) Approve a rate or regulation; 
(ii) Adopt or enforce a law;

order, or similar authorization or entitlement; 
(iv) Authorize the City to enter into, modify, or renew a contract provided it is the type of 
contract that requires agency approval; 
(v) Grant agency approval to a contract that requires agency approval and to which the 
agency is a party, or to the specifications for such a contract; 
(vi) Grant agency approval to a plan, design, report, study or similar item; 
(vii) Adopt, or grant agency approval of policies, standards, or guidelines for the City. 
You have stated that you will be preparing the report at the City's request, and that 
rejection is not an option. The report designates policies, standards, or guidelines for the City. 
This means that your report carries the weight of adopting a policy, standard, or guideline for the 
City. You are consultant under the Act. 
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental 
decision? 
A public official makes a governmental decision when, acting within the authority of his 
or her position, the official votes on a matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her 
agency to any particular course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of 
his or her agency, or determines not to act. (Regulation 18702.1 (a).) A public official 
participates in a governmental decision when, acting within the authority of his or her position,

City's request, you will be preparing policies, standards, or guidelines that they will not reject. 
Preparing the report is making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision. 
Step Three: What are your financial interests? 
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial interest in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effects on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 


immediate family, or on any of the following five enumerated interests:  
• An interest in any business entity if the official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1 (a)), or if the official is a director, 
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management in the business 
entity. (Section 87103.l(d); Regulation 18703.l(b)). 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.) 
• An interest in a source of non-gift income or promised income, which aggregates to $500 
or more within the 12 months prior to the governmental decision. (Section 87103(c); 
Regulation 18703.3.) 
• An interest in any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of gifts that 
aggregate to a fair market value of $440 or more within 12 months prior to the 
governmental decision. (Section 87103(e). Regulation 18703.4.) 
• An interest in the official's personal finances or the finances of the official's immediate 
family. The "personal financial effects" rule finds interest where a governmental 
decision will increase or decrease the personal income, expenses, assets, or liabilities of 
the official or their immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation l 8703.5.) 
Real Property: 
By all indications, your property interest is valued at $2,000 or more. If so, you have a 
potentially disqualifying interest in real property. (Section 87103(b).) Because you have not 
provided facts indicating any other potential financial interest, our analysis is limited to your real 
property interest. 
Business Interest: 
By all indications, you have an ownership interest of at least 10% in your company, and 
that ownership interest is worth $2,000 or more. You therefore have an interest in the business 
entity under Section 87103(d). However, the facts presented do not indicate that your business is 
the subject of your decisions with respect to the MAD Report drafting. Without further

potential conflicts that may arise because of your business. 
You also have an interest in clients who provide, based on a proportionate share of your 
business income, $500 or more in income to you during a 12-month period prior to your 
participation in the MAD Report drafting. You have not provided sufficient facts for us to 
analyze these potential sources for conflicts of interest. As mentioned, we cannot analyze 
potential conflicts that may arise because of your business. 


Source of Income:  
It also appears that you have received income from your business of $500 or more in the 
last 12 months prior to the MAD Report drafting. If so, you will have an interest in your 
business as a source of income. (Section 87103(c).) As with the analysis of your business 
interest, we cannot analyze this potential conflict without more information regarding the process 
by which an engineering firm is selected for MAD Report drafting. 
Personal Finances: 
A public official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. A governmental 
decision will have an effect on this interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, 
income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or 
decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) However, a financial effect on the gross 
revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which a public official 
has a direct or indirect investment interest is not considered a separate financial effect on an 
official's personal finances and would not be analyzed separately under the "personal financial 
effects" rule. (Regulation 18705.S(a).) 
It is unclear from the facts whether the savings incurred by the use of your own 
engineering firm will benefit your personal finances or those of a business entity. While we 
cannot analyze your specific situation, we can offer general information regarding this potential 
conflict. 
Step Four: Is your real property interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision? 
To determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a 
given interest is material, you must first determine if the interest is directly involved or indirectly 
involved in the governmental decision. (Regulation I 8704(a).) 
Real Property: 
Regulation 18704.2 provides, in part: 
"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is 
directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply: 
"(I) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of 
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries ( or the 
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the 
governmental decision."  
Your property is within the MAD, and is therefore within 500 feet of the proposed 
boundaries. Accordingly, unless an exception applies, your real property interest is directly 
involved in the real property governmental decisions regarding the MAD Report under 
Regulation 18704.2(a)( 1 ). 
In your request, you mention several exceptions that you think may apply in your case.

see that any exceptions apply to the facts you provide. 
Regulation 18702.4(a)(3) allows a consultant to participate in a governmental decision 
regarding his or her compensation in a private capacity. Distinguishing private capacity does not 
exempt your participation, but merely distinguishes between the different hats that you would 
need to wear when participating in a governmental decision. Applied to your facts, you would 
be making, participating in making, or using your position to influence a decision if you take part 
in drafting the MAD Report, but would not be involved with governmental decisions when you 
are solely representing yourself as the property owner. (Reed Advice Letter, No. 1-13-091.)

recuses him or herself from participating in the governmental decision in his or her public 
capacity. The exception would not allow you to be involved with the MAD Report outside of 
any public meetings. (See Mitchell Advice Letter, supra and Simonian Advice Letter, No. A-08-
096.) It allows you only to participate in public meetings in the same way as any member of the 
public would. 
Regulation l 8702.4(b )( 4) carves out an exception for architectural or engineering 
submissions and drawings. This exception is limited to the preparation of technical documents 
by public officials in their private capacity. (Grocott Advice Letter, No. A-02-028; Smith 
Advice Letter, No. A-96-022; Dorsey Advice Letter, No. A-94-284.) This exception does not 
apply to the MAD Report process, because you are preparing the MAD Report in your capacity 
as a consultant. In a memorandum to the Commission in support of the Regulation I 8702.4, 
staff explained the exception's purpose: 
"The cities were basically concerned about limitations on the professional 
activities of planning commissioners and city council members who are architects 
and engineers. There was general agreement that planning commissioner/ 
architects should not actually represent clients before the planning commission. It  
was also agreed that such architects and engineers should be able to prepare the 
drawings and plans for clients, even though the drawings and the plans would 
have to be submitted to the planning commission. There seemed to be general 
consensus among the city attorneys that this exception is necessary in order to 
allow the local architects and engineers to continue to practice their profession 
when they act as planning commissioners or council members." (Staff 
Memorandum to the Commission, dated April 29, 1985.) 
Based on the facts provided, Regulation I 8702.4(a)( I) may be relevant. That regulation 
provides an exception for actions of public officials that are solely ministerial, secretarial, 
manual, or clerical. If you exercise no discretion in drafting the MAD Report, then 
18702.4(a)(l) applies. Your letter indicates that there can be only one outcome with respect to 
the MAD Report. But you also indicated during our telephone conversation that there are 
industry standards by which you measure the cost of each necessary component in the 
development plans. Industry standards are not the constraints contemplated by the regulation. 
The regulation may only apply if there are constraints to the use of any discretion. Please keep 
in mind that it is irrelevant that you are legally constrained in some action by the Business and 
Professional Code. The Act's determinations regarding a conflict of interest are not informed by 
ethics enforcement mechanisms in other code sections. If you are able to exercise any discretion 
in drafting the MAD Report, then the regulation cannot apply. 
Personal Finances: 
An official's interest in his or her personal finances is deemed to be directly involved in 
the governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect on his or her personal finances. 
(Regulation 18704.5.) 
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your 
real property interest? 
A conflict-of-interest arises only where a material effect on an official's economic 
interest is reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation I 8700(a).) 
Materiality 
Real Property: 
Any financial effect, even one penny, of a governmental decision on real property 
directly involved in the governmental decision is presumed to be material. (Regulation 
I 8705.2(a)(I ).) This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable 
that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real property, irrespective of 
the impact. (Ibid.) Even a one penny change qualifies as a financial effect. Your real property is 
directly involved with the MAD Report drafting, and is presumed material. 


Personal Finances:  
An effect on an official's personal finances is material as stated in Regulation 
18705.S(a), which provides: 
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal finances 
is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period." 
Reasonable Foreseeability 
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to his or her 
interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the standard 
will be met. A material financial effect on an interest need not be certain or even substantially 
likely to be "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (Regulation 
18706(a); /11 re Thomer (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) 
Real Property: 
Because your property is the subject of the MAD Report, and because the MAD Report 
will distribute the costs of development and compliance to you, there is almost surely more than 
a mere possibility that drafting the MAD Report will have a one penny impact on the value of 
your real property. Unless you can prove that there will be no reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on your real property value as a result of the MAD Report, your interest will 
disqualify you from participating in drafting the Report. 
Personal Finances: 
Based upon the facts provided, you will save approximately $10,000 by using your own 
firm. It is unclear whether that money is saved by you or a business entity. If the drafting of the 
MAD report results in a benefit to your personal finances of at least $250, then your drafting the 
MAD Report will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your personal finances. 
Steps Seven and Eight: Does the governmental decision come within any exception to the 
conflict-of-interest rules? 
Public Generally 
The material financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic 
interest in real property is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if that decision 
also affects ten percent or more of all property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency, 
or 5,000 property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency. (Regulation 18707.1 (a); 
Regulation 18707.1 (b )(B)(i)-(ii).)  
The contemplated MAD encompasses and affects only the four acres that you own and a 
very small amount of public land. The public generally exception does not apply. 
Legally Required Participation 
There are a limited number of circumstances that allow a public official to take part in a 
governmental decision despite a disqualifying conflict of interest under the legally required 
participation exception. This exception applies very rarely, and only where the government 
agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section 8710 l; Regulation 18708.) 
You have indicated that there are at least two other engineering firms that are capable of 
completing the MAD Report, albeit at significantly higher cost. There is no prohibition based on 
cost. The legally required participation exception does not apply.

QUESTION: May you prepare a Municipal Assessment District Engineers Report for a project that 
covers the development of your real property?

CONCLUSION: No. As discussed below, it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will 
have a material financial effect on your real property. 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18 I 10 through 18 997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Donald M. Jensen, P.E. C.E.O. Jensen Design and Survey, Inc. 1672 Donlon Street Ventura, CA 93003 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-13-138 Dear Mr. Jensen: November 15, 2013 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. (/11 re Oglesby (1975), 1 FPPC Ops. 7 l.) Please note that all advice is based only on the provisions of the Act. We offer no opinion on the application of other laws that may apply, such as Government Code Section 1090 or common law conflict of interest. Also, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. QUESTION May you prepare a Municipal Assessment District Engineers Report for a project that covers the development of your real property? CONCLUSION No. As discussed below, it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on your real property. 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9l014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission arc contained in Sections 18 I 10 through 18 997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. FACTS File No. A-13-138 Page No. 2 You are an engineer in the city of Ventura and you own a private engineering firm. Your firm contracts with the City on an occasional basis lo provide engineering design and other services. You own approximately four acres of real property subdivided, zoned, and prepared for residential development in Ventura. The subdivision has all discretionary approvals, and a vesting tentative map is fully approved. One required condition for recordation of the final map is the City's formation and acceptance of a Maintenance Assessment District. To create these districts, the City contracts with an engineering consulting firm to draft a Maintenance Assessment District Report (MAD Report). The MAD Report establishes a district around the proposed project, calculates and distributes various costs for public land, taxes, and legal compliance with various sections of local and state law. You indicate that acceptance of a MAD Report is assumed, and that rejection is not an option for the City. A MAD Report must be drafted, and it is lo your financial benefit that you prepare it. You prepare such reports as a matter of course for your clients, have extensive knowledge of the process and
